segments.s318-g105: This is a short clause of sequential action: بپردخت and نهاد are past verbs describing what 'he' did. زان پس is a temporal connector 'after that' (زان = 'from that', پس = 'after/then'). به رامش marks the indirect object or destination ('to/into Ramash'). نهاد is the past of نهادن 'to place/put' and completes the action of delivering someone or something to Ramash.
segments.s318-g106: برفتند is the simple past plural 'they departed/went away'. گردان is a collective noun 'troop/retinue' and is the subject; خسرو نژاد is a compound/name (Khosrow‑nezhad) modifying that troop. Word order is verb first for emphasis then subject (common in epic Persian), so 'they departed, the troop of Khosrow‑nezhad.'
segments.s318-g107: هر آن کس که بود is a relative/free‑choice clause 'whoever/any person who was'. از نژاد introduces origin/lineage ('of the lineage/race of ...'). زرسب names that lineage (here functioning as an epithet or clan name). The clause sets a condition for the following verb بیامد.
segments.s318-g108: بیامد is the simple past 'came'. به marks direction 'to', and ایوان آذرگشسب is the place ('the iwan/porch of Azar‑Gashasp'). This is a straightforward past narrative clause completing the previous 'whoever ...' construction.
segments.s318-g109: چهل روز begins the time span 'forty days'. با شاه کاووس attaches the companion 'with King Kavus'. کی here is an interrogative/relative 'who' (i.e., 'whoever it was who was with the king for forty days'); the clause sets up the identification of that person.
segments.s318-g110: همی بود is an imperfective past marker in epic style meaning 'was continually/always'. با introduces the accompaniment list: رامش (a person/companion), رود (here an instrument, 'the lute' or stringed instrument in poetic usage), و می 'and wine'. The clause lists with whom/what he spent those days.
segments.s318-g111: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. رخشنده شد is a past‑tense verbal phrase 'became radiant/gleamed' (رخشنده = 'radiant', شد = 'became'). بر فلک ماه نو places the subject in the sky: 'the new moon on the heaven/sky' — literal order is 'became radiant upon the sky the new moon'.
segments.s318-g112: This is a nominal/visual clause: ز زر افسری ('a diadem of gold' — ز here is poetic 'of/from') بر سر ('on the head of') شاه نو ('the new king'). The verb is omitted (typical in poetry) and understood as 'was placed' or 'rested'. افسری = diadem/helmet, زر = gold.
segments.s318-g113: بزرگان is the subject 'the great ones / nobles' (plural). سوی + پارس is a directional prepositional phrase 'toward Pars' (سوی before the place). کردند روی is a poetic/periphrastic expression: کردند (past plural 'they did/made') + روی ('face') together mean 'they turned (their) face(s)' toward the stated direction — a common classical Persian way to say 'they turned toward.'
segments.s318-g114: برآسوده is a predicate/adverbial 'at ease, relieved'. از introduces the source or cause 'from / free of'. رزم is 'battle, war'. وز here functions as the poetic contraction 'و ز' linking two nouns (i.e. 'and from'); this usage is archaic/poetic rather than the literal senses listed below. گفتگوی is 'conversation / dispute' and completes the phrase 'free from battle and from dispute.'
segments.s318-g115: به هر شهر is 'to/into every city' (به + هر + noun). کاندر is a fused relative/adverbial 'in which / wherein' referring back to شهر. شدندی is an archaic past plural form meaning 'they came/arrived / became' (here 'they arrived into' or 'they were in'), and ز راه 'from/by the road' specifies the route — overall: 'into every city in which they arrived by the road.'
segments.s318-g116: شدی here is an intransitive past/clausal verb meaning 'there was formed / it happened' (passive/impersonal sense). انجمن مرد is the noun phrase 'an assembly of men' (انجمن = assembly, مرد = men). بر پیشگاه is a locative/scene-setting phrase 'before the presence / at the court' (بر + پیشگاه): together the clause says an assembly of men came together before the court.
segments.s318-g117: گشادی سر بدره‌ها is a descriptive/prepositional attributive phrase placed before شهریار: گشادی 'broadness / wide' qualifying سر 'head / face', بدره‌ها 'the full moons' (a poetic simile). Grammatically this premodifier describes the king's appearance — 'the king with a head/face broad as the full moons.'
segments.s318-g118: توانگر شدی is 'became wealthy' (توانگر = wealthy; شدی = became). مرد پرهیزگار is the resulting predicate nominal 'a pious man' (مرد = man/person, پرهیزگار = pious). Word order places the quality (توانگر) and verb (شدی) before the noun phrase that names the outcome (مرد پرهیزگار): 'he became a wealthy, pious man.'
segments.s318-g9: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase 'gave (this) answer/answered thus' (چنین = 'thus/so' + داد = 'gave' + پاسخ = 'answer'). The following که‌ای is a compressed classical sequence: که (introducing reported speech 'that') + ای (vocative particle), so the speaker immediately addresses بدکنش ('O ill-doer'). Word order is typical Persian: predicate first ('gave an answer thus'), then the content of the answer introduced by که and the vocative.
segments.s318-g10: سزاوار پیغاره و سرزنش is a nominal predicate following the vocative: سزاوار 'deserving of' governs پیغاره 'a message/notice/admonition' and then و + سرزنش 'and reproach'. The construction means 'deserving of admonition and reproach' and lacks an explicit copula because Persian often omits it in predicates.
segments.s318-g11: ز جان برادرت گویم نخست is an adverbial/parenthetical opening: ز + جان + برادرت = 'for/from your brother's life' (ز here = 'for / on account of', جان = 'life', برادرت = 'your brother' with second-person suffix -ت). گویم is 1st person singular (I say / I will say) in literary Persian and نخست 'first' orders the sequence of what the speaker will state.
segments.s318-g12: که introduces reported content 'that ...'. هرگز 'never' negates the verb نجست, and بلای مهان را is the object phrase: بلای 'calamity/affliction' + مهان 'guests' with object marker را. نجست is a past tense verb here meaning 'sought' (negated by هرگز → 'never sought'). The clause thus says he never sought calamity for/against the guests.
segments.s318-g13: دگر نوذر آن نامور شهریار is a nominative noun group: دگر 'another' precedes the proper name نوذر (Nowzar), then the demonstrative/article آن 'that' and epithets نامور 'famed' شهریار 'king'. Word order is typical: determiner/adjective(s) follow or precede the name in classical Persian style.
segments.s318-g14: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. از تخم ایرج = 'from the seed of Irāj' (از = 'from/of', تخم = 'seed/lineage', ایرج = proper name). بد او یادگار is a compact predicate: بد 'bad/ill' modifies یادگار 'memorial/remembrance' with او 'him' as the person to whom the remembrance relates; literally 'an ill remembrance to him' (i.e. an unfortunate legacy).
segments.s318-g15: زدی گردنش را به شمشیر تیز is a transitive past clause: زدی = (you) struck/attacked (2nd person past), گردنش = 'his neck' (گردن + possessive suffix ش = 'his'), را = object marker, به شمشیر تیز = 'with a sharp sword' (به + instrument + adjective). Word order puts verb first (classical narrative past) and the instrument phrase after the object.
segments.s318-g16: برانگیختی is 2nd person past ('you aroused/raised/provoked'), and از جهان رستخیز is a prepositional/nominal complement: از = 'from/in/through', جهان = 'the world', رستخیز = 'uprising/resurrection' used here as 'uproar/uprising'. Together the clause means 'you provoked an upheaval in the world' (classical poetic inversion of cause and effect).
segments.s318-g33: کنون (adverb) 'now' starts the clause; روز (noun) here means 'time/era' rather than the literal daylight. بادافره is a poetic adjective ('wind‑borne, fleeting') modifying روز, and ایزدیست is the archaic fused form of 'ایزدی است' (is divine/of God) with the copula attached — word order is inverted for poetic effect: 'Now is a fleeting/divine time.'
segments.s318-g34: مکافات is the main noun 'retribution/punishment' with بد in genitive position giving 'punishment of evil'; را is the emphatic/definite object marker placed after that noun phrase. ز (poetic for از) introduces the source 'from God' (یزدان). بدیست is the fused older form of 'بدی است' ('is bad/harsh') used idiomatically here to say that the punishment of evil comes from God and is severe — the copular adjective follows the source phrase in poetic word order.
segments.s318-g35: به before an instrument here means 'by/with' (instrumental). شمشیر هندی is a noun + adjectival postmodifier 'Indian sword' (adjective follows in poetic/literary usage). بزد is the simple past third‑person 'he struck/smote'. گردن + ش is the possessed noun 'his neck' (the possessive enclitic ش attached). The phrase is straightforward subject‑verb‑object with the instrument fronted for emphasis.
segments.s318-g36: به here marks direction 'to/into' ('to the earth'). خاک اندر افگند is a compact poetic sequence meaning 'threw/cast into the dust/earth' (اندر = 'into', افگند = past 'threw'). نازک تنش is an inverted noun phrase: نازک 'delicate/slender' modifies تن 'body', with the possessive ش attached — 'his delicate body' was cast into the dust.
segments.s318-g37: ز introduces the agent/source 'by/from the blood' (خون). لعل شد is 'became ruby' (لعل 'ruby', شد 'became'); the noun phrase ریش و موی سپید ('white beard and hair') is placed after the verb for poetic inversion, so the sense is that his formerly white beard and hair were reddened/stained like ruby by blood.
segments.s318-g38: برادرش is 'his brother' with possessive ش. گشت is past 'became/turned' (here 'became' intransitive). از جهان ناامید is a prepositional phrase 'hopeless about the world' (از = 'about/of/regarding', جهان = 'the world/this life', ناامید = 'hopeless') — together: 'his brother became hopeless concerning the world' (i.e., despaired).
segments.s318-g39: تهی ماند is a compound meaning 'remained empty/was left void' (تهی = empty, ماند = remained/was left). ز او means 'of him' or 'from him' (devoid of him). گاه شاهنشهی is a genitive/compound 'the place/time of kingship' (گاه 'place/time', شاهنشهی 'imperial rule'); poetic order places the modifying phrase after the verb phrase.
segments.s318-g40: سرآمد here means 'the end/conclusion' (not 'foremost') and governs the clause; بر او = 'upon/for him'. روزگار مهی is 'a moon‑like / beautiful time' (روزگار = time/fate, مهی = 'moon‑like, beautiful'). The phrase thus says that the beautiful days reached their end for him — poetic inversion places سرآمد before روزگار.
segments.s318-g81: بر آن موبدان خلعت افگند نیز is a compact clause where the prepositional phrase بر آن موبدان ('upon those mobeds') precedes the object خلعت ('a robe of honour') and the verb افگند (past) follows. افگند is an older/poetic past form literally meaning 'threw' but here idiomatically 'bestowed' (i.e. cast/placed a robe on them). نیز is an adverb 'also/too' linking this action to previous gifts.
segments.s318-g82: درم داد و دینار و بسیار چیز lists gifts: 'he gave dirhams and dinars and many things.' داد is the past of دادن 'gave' and governs the preceding direct object درم; the following items (دینار و بسیار چیز) are additional objects coordinated by و, so the single verb داد applies to them all.
segments.s318-g83: به شهر اندرون هر که درویش بود is a locative + relative clause: به شهر اندرون ('into/within the city's inner quarters') sets the place, and هر که ... بود ('whoever was') introduces the relative/indefinite subject. درویش here means a poor/dervish person, and بود is the past copula 'was.' Word order places the location first, then the indefinite subject clause.
segments.s318-g84: وگر خوردش از کوشش خویش بود is a conditional/alternative phrase: وگر ('if' / 'or if') introduces the condition; خوردش is the past of خوردن 'to eat' with the enclitic object -ش ('his/it'), here idiomatically meaning 'lived/eat' (i.e. earned his living). از کوشش خویش ('from his own effort') gives the source/instrument of his sustenance, and بود is the past copula completing the clause.
segments.s318-g85: بر آن نیز گنجی پراگنده کرد follows the same pattern as earlier: بر آن ('there/on that') + نیز ('also') specify location/continuation; گنجی ('a treasure') is the direct object and پراگنده کرد is the verb phrase 'scattered' (پراگنده + کرد, where کرد completes/causativizes the action). The object-before-verb order is normal in this style.
segments.s318-g86: جهانی به داد و دهش بنده کرد is a causative statement: جهانی ('the whole world' / universally) is the object made into بنده ('a servant'); به introduces the means/instrument ('by') and links داد و دهش ('giving and grants') as the means. کرد here carries a causative sense 'made/caused to be.'
segments.s318-g87: از آن پس به تخت کیان برنشست is built from the temporal phrase از آن پس ('after that'), followed by the locative/goal به تخت کیان ('to/on the throne of the Kians') and the past verb برنشست ('he sat upon/took his seat'). برنشست is the past of برنشستن (prefix بر- + نشستن) meaning 'sat upon' — a classical/poetic compound verb.
segments.s318-g88: در بار بگشاد و لب را ببست contains two coordinated actions. در بار ('in the court' — بار here = royal court/audience) + بگشاد (an archaic/past form meaning 'he opened' or 'he held open') means 'he opened his court/received people.' The second clause لب را ببست ('he closed/shut his lip(s)') uses را as the definite object marker and ببست (past) meaning 'he closed,' idiomatically 'kept silent.'
segments.s318-g73: ز یزدان is a prepositional source phrase ('from/by God') placed before the temporal marker چو. چو here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'. شاه آرزوها is a poetic genitive: شاه (king) + آرزوها (wishes) = 'the king's wishes' without an explicit ezāfe. بیافت is an archaic past-type verb meaning 'befell' / 'were fulfilled' and agrees with the plural notion of آرزوها (what happened to the wishes).
segments.s318-g74: The clause continues with the main action: ز دریا 'from the sea' gives origin, سوی 'toward' marks direction, and خان آذر is a titled name (Khan Azar). شتافت is past tense 'hastened' (3rd person); its subject is the king mentioned in the previous segment, carried over by the narrative.
segments.s318-g75: بسی زر is an indefinite quantifying phrase 'much/great amount of gold' serving as the object. بر آتش is a prepositional phrase 'upon the fire'. برافشاندند is a past plural verb 'they cast/threw' (poetic past), with the plural subject understood from the surrounding scene.
segments.s318-g76: به زمزم places the action 'at/over the Zamzam (sacred water)'. همی is an archaic, habitual/continuous particle (here 'repeatedly' / 'kept on'). آفرین is the exclamation or acclamation 'afarin' (praise) and خواندند is past plural 'they uttered/chant(ed) it' (here 'they chanted' or 'cried out').
segments.s318-g77: ببودند is an archaic past plural 'they were/they remained'. یک روز و یک شب is a duration phrase 'one day and one night' (object of duration). به پای is a locative phrase 'at the feet' (i.e., in attendance/at the foot of someone). The verb governs the temporal and locative complements.
segments.s318-g78: به پیش means 'before' or 'in the presence of'. جهانداور رهنمای are two epithets placed in apposition (no ezāfe in the poem): 'the world-judge, the guide' — both modify the person before whom they remained. This is a poetic compact apposition common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s318-g79: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. گنجور is an epithet 'treasure-laden/treasury-bearing' describing کیخسرو (Ki-Khosrow) as a compound noun phrase. آمد is simple past 'came/arrived'. زرسب is another qualifying epithet ('golden') applied in the same poetic string of epithets; the ordering is flexible in poetry.
segments.s318-g80: ببخشید is a past tense verb 'he gave/bestowed' (here 3rd-person). گنجی is the direct object 'a treasure' (indefinite). بر here marks the recipient with the sense 'to/toward' (poetic usage), and آذرگشسب is the proper name of the recipient (Azar-Gashasp). The clause thus reports the king's giving of a treasure to Azar-Gashasp.
segments.s318-g97: روان سیاووش را is a genitive/possessive NP meaning “the soul of Siavash” with the definite/object marker را attached after the name; زنده کرد is a compound predicate where the adjective زنده (“alive”) combines with the light verb کرد (“made/did”) to yield the causative ‘revived’ or ‘brought to life.’ Word order (NP then predicate) is normal for Persian prose/poetic narration.
segments.s318-g98: جهان را is the definite object “the world.” به introduces an instrumental/adverbial phrase here (‘by/through’). داد و دهش are two abstract nouns joined by و meaning “justice and giving/generosity,” and بنده کرد is ‘made (into) a servant’ where بنده is predicate nominal and کرد is the light verb ‘made.’ Overall: ‘made the world a servant by justice and generosity.’
segments.s318-g99: همی is an adverb signaling habitual/continuous action (“continually/always”) placed before the verb. چیز is the (indefinite/general) thing(s) given, and بخشید is the past of بخشیدن used here with the meaning ‘gave/bestowed.’ درویش را is the recipient with را marking it as a definite/particular beneficiary. The sequence expresses a repeated act of giving to the dervish.
segments.s318-g100: پرستنده و مردم خویش را continues the list of beneficiaries: two coordinated nouns (پرستنده “worshipper/devotee” and مردم “people”) followed by the possessive clitic خوی+ش (here written as خوی ش) meaning ‘his/their own.’ The trailing را is the same object-marking particle that links this whole coordinated NP back to the verb بخشید earlier, so these are additional recipients.
segments.s318-g101: از آن پس is a temporal phrase ‘after that’ (از + demonstrative آن + پس). چنین modifies گفت to mean ‘thus said.’ In this line the verb phrase چنین گفت appears before the subject شاه جهان (verb–subject order), a rhetorical inversion common in poetry — literally ‘thus said the king of the world.’
segments.s318-g102: که‌ای here introduces the following direct address/exclamation (classical vocative equivalent to ‘O’). نامداران is a plural noun ‘the renowned/famed ones,’ فرخ is an adjective ‘fortunate/auspicious,’ and مهان is a vocative epithet (literary/archaic) applied to those addressed; together they form a string of epithets in direct address (‘O famed, fortunate nobles/guests’). The word order places the epithets together after the vocative particle.
segments.s318-g103: زن و کودک خرد is a coordinated subject ‘women and small children’ (خرد is the adjective ‘small/little’ modifying کودک). بیرون is an adverb meaning ‘out/outside,’ and برید is a past verb here meaning ‘went/left’ (classical/poetic usage of برید as ‘departed/went forth’). The verb follows the adverb and agrees with the plural subject.
segments.s318-g104: خورشها و رامش are two coordinated proper names/nominal groups (here treated as agents); به introduces the destination (‘to’), هامون is the place ‘hamun/plain,’ and برید (same verb as above) means ‘went/advanced’ — so the line says these named parties went to the plain. In classical Persian narrative, proper names appear without overt case marking and the verb follows the place phrase.
segments.s318-g49: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' placed initially for emphasis. گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (verb before subject here is a poetic inversion). موبد is the subject 'the mobed' (Zoroastrian priest). به + بهرام تیز marks the indirect object 'to Bahram Tiz', where تیز is an epithet 'the Swift/keen' attached after the name.
segments.s318-g50: که introduces the reported clause ('that' / introducing direct speech/command). خون سر بیگناهان is a genitive chain 'the blood (خون) of the heads (سر) of the innocent (بیگناهان)'—a poetic way to say 'the innocent's blood'. مریز is the negative imperative 'do not spill/pour' (م‑ prefix + present stem ریز) addressing Bahram.
segments.s318-g51: چو is the conditional 'if'. خواهی is 2nd person present 'you want/wish'. که introduces the subordinate clause of desire. تاج تو is 'your crown' (possessive). ماند is a jussive/subjunctive-like form 'may remain' / 'remain' (poetic third person). به جای = 'in its place'.
segments.s318-g52: مبادی is the plural noun 'approaches/means/ways' (here: methods to keep the crown). جز is the exclusion 'except'. آهسته و پاک‌رای are qualities modifying مبادی: آهسته 'slow/gentle' (used adjectivally/adverbially) and پاک‑رای 'of pure intention / upright in reputation'. The phrase means 'no means except gentle and pure‑minded ones.'
segments.s318-g53: نگه کن is an imperative 'look/attend/pay attention' (نگه = look/watch, کن = imperative marker). که introduces a content clause 'that/what'. خود تاج 'the crown itself' (خود emphatic). با سر here means 'to the head' or 'with the head' (با marking the interlocutor in a speech). چه گفت is a fused interrogative/indirect 'what it said' (چه = what, گفت = said).
segments.s318-g54: که again introduces the reported content/optative clause. با مغزت = 'with/paired to your brain' (مغزت = your brain). ای is the vocative 'O', addressing سر خرد 'wise head' (سر = head, خرد = wisdom). باد here is the optative particle 'may (it be)' and جفت is predicate 'paired' (so: 'May it be paired with your brain, O wise head').
segments.s318-g55: به گرسیوز آمد is a verb‑first poetic order 'came to Garsivaz' (آمد = came, به + name = to). ز کار means 'from the affair/that business'. نیا here functions as a personal name or agent that arrived (textual/onomastic usage); word order puts the verb before the coming person, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s318-g56: دو رخ زرد is a compact descriptive phrase: دو رخ 'two faces/cheeks' + زرد 'pale' = 'two pale cheeks' (i.e. a pale countenance). و یک دل پر از کیمیا = 'and a single heart full of kīmiyā' where پر از = 'full of' and کیمیا (literally 'alchemy' or 'elixir') is used metaphorically (here: secret or guileful purpose).
segments.s318-g25: بمان is an imperative (second-person singular) formed from the verb 'ماندن' meaning 'remain/stay'. تا introduces a subordinate purpose/temporal clause that connects forward to the verb ببینم; مگر here functions as an intensifying/introducing particle (classical usage) that nuances the purpose (not the modern senses 'except' or 'but'); مادرت is مادَر + enclitic -ت 'your' and را is the definite object marker attaching to مادرت; به جان is a fixed collocation meaning 'alive / with life' (به + جان), placed after the object as a postpositional phrase that modifies the seeing expressed by the clause's verb.
segments.s318-g26: ببینم is the first-person singular present-subjunctive/jussive ('that I may see' / 'let me see') formed from the present stem بین- plus the suffix -م; پس here is transitional/temporal 'then' linking the seeing clause to the following imperative; این داستانها بخوان is an imperative clause where بخوان (second-person singular imperative) commands 'recite/read' and داستانها is the plural direct object marked implicitly by word order (را was used earlier); the sequence shows: remain — so that I may see your mother — then do this.
segments.s318-g27: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (بـ + او), introducing the indirect speech recipient. گفت‌گر should be read as گفت + گر (i.e. 'he said: if...') — classical Persian often fuses گفت and the conditional particle گر; خواستی is the second-person form 'you wished/you wanted' (here conditional: 'if you wished'), and مادرم is 'my mother' (مادر + enclitic -م). The clause sets up reported speech with an embedded conditional introduced by گر after گفت.
segments.s318-g28: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. آتش is the direct object 'fire.' افروختی is the second-person singular past tense 'you kindled' (perfective); the verb directly takes the object آتش, and بر is the preposition 'upon/on' governing سرم, where سر (head) carries the possessive suffix -م 'my' to form 'my head'. The whole phrase is a direct question embedded in reported speech.
segments.s318-g29: پدر is the subject 'father.' بیگنه (classical form of بیگانه) means 'strange, foreign, unfamiliar' and describes the father. بود is the past copula 'was.' و coordinates the next clause; من در نهان is 'I (was) in secret / I was hidden' where در نهان is a locative/adverbial phrase ('in secret'). Word order is Subject – Predicate – and then a coordinated personal clause.
segments.s318-g30: چه is the interrogative 'what' (here introducing an exclamatory/interrogative clause). رفت is the past-tense verb 'went' or idiomatically 'befell/ happened to.' از is the preposition 'from' marking source; گزند means 'harm' or 'injury'; تو is the second-person pronoun 'you'; اندر جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world'/'in this life'. Together the clause asks rhetorically what harm the speaker suffered from you in the world.
segments.s318-g31: سر شهریاری is a genitival/compound phrase 'the head/chief of kingship/sovereignty' (سر here = 'chief/top'). ربودی is a perfective verb 'you seized/robbed/stole' (second-person past); که is the relative/complementizer 'that' introducing a following clause (continues onto the next segment). Word order places the object phrase (سر شهریاری) fronted for emphasis before the verb ربودی.
segments.s318-g32: بدو here is 'to it/him' (i.e. 'unto him/it'). زار is an adverb/adjective meaning 'wretched/lamentsome' modifying گریان شد; گریان is a participial/adjectival form 'weeping,' and شد is the past/copular 'became' (i.e. 'began to weep' / 'was rendered weeping'). و is the conjunction 'and' linking the next noun phrase تخت عاج 'ivory throne' (تخت = 'throne', عاج = 'ivory'), which completes the image. The structure is verb phrase describing reaction followed by a coordinated noun phrase.
segments.s318-g17: This phrase names Siavash and begins a relative clause. 'سه دیگر' literally uses 'دیگر' (other/another) combined with 'سه' (here functioning idiomatically as 'no other / none other' in the poetic context) to mark uniqueness; the name 'سیاوش' follows. The relative particle 'که' introduces the clause 'که چون او سوار…' (‘who, for none like him as a rider…’), and 'چون او سوار' is a simile/comparison 'a rider like him' with 'چون' preceding the pronoun + noun.
segments.s318-g18: 'نبیند' is a poetic/archaic present-tense negative form meaning here '(has) seen not / no one saw' — effectively 'no one has seen' or 'no one saw.' 'کسی' is used in the sense 'no one' (negative polarity), 'از مهان' is a genitive/partitive 'of/from the guests/nobles,' and 'یادگار' is the predicate noun 'a remembrance / a thing to be remembered' — together: 'no one among the guests has seen (one) like him as a remembrance.'
segments.s318-g19: 'بریدی' is 2nd-person singular past tense 'you cut' (classical/past narrative). The object 'سرش' is head + enclitic third-person possessive 'his.' The simile 'چون سر گوسفند' places 'چون' before the noun phrase to mean 'like the head of a sheep.' Word order: verb — object — simile phrase.
segments.s318-g20: 'همی' is a poetic particle (here emphatic/temporal, 'then/indeed'), and 'برگذشتی' is 2nd-person past 'you passed / you departed.' The preposition 'ز' (poetic form of 'از') marks source 'from,' and 'چرخ بلند' is a compound noun phrase 'the lofty sky/heaven' (چرخ often = heaven/firmament or fate). Together this yields 'then you passed away from the lofty heaven.'
segments.s318-g21: 'به' here introduces the manner/field ('in' or 'with') and combines with 'کردار بد' = 'bad conduct / evil deed.' 'تیز' is an adverb 'swift(ly)' placed before the verb, and 'بشتافتی' is 2nd-person past (or perfective) 'you hastened / you rushed.' The phrase describes the manner of the action: 'you rushed on in wicked conduct.'
segments.s318-g22: 'مکافات' is the noun 'retribution / recompense.' 'آن بد' = 'that bad/evil (deed)' (demonstrative + adjective), 'کنون' = 'now,' and 'یافتی' is 2nd-person past of 'یافتن' used as 'you have obtained/received.' Together: 'now you have received the retribution for that evil.'
segments.s318-g23: 'بدو' is the enclitic prepositional phrase 'to him.' 'گفت' is the verbal root 'said' (here rendered as narrative 'he said'), and 'شاها' is the vocative 'O King.' 'ببود' is an archaic/past form 'was' (often emphatic or narrative past), and 'آنچه بود' = 'that which was / what has been.' The whole segment is direct speech introduction plus the formulaic 'what has been, has been.'
segments.s318-g24: 'کنون' = 'now.' 'داستانم' is 'my story/tale' (داستان + possessive -م). 'بباید' is an impersonal/modal form 'it is necessary / must,' and 'شنود' is an archaic subjunctive/infinitival form 'hear' (here: 'must hear' — i.e. 'you must listen'). The clause literally reads 'now my story must be heard' (understood: by you, the king).
segments.s318-g89: نبشتند is a past 3rd-person-plural verb (نوشتند in New Persian; here an archaic/poetic form) meaning 'they wrote'. It appears sentence-initial by poetic inversion, followed by its object نامه ('letter'), and then the dative phrase به هر کشوری ('to every country'), where به marks the indirect object/recipient and هر+کشوری means 'each/ every country'.
segments.s318-g90: This repeats the dative marker به to list additional recipients: 'to every نامداری and every مهتری.' هر before each noun is the distributive 'every/each'. نامداری here denotes a distinguished person (a 'notable'), and مهتری denotes a chief/elder—both recipients of the letters.
segments.s318-g91: ز خاور is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the East' (ز = 'from'). نامه is the theme 'the letter'. بشد is a past/inchoative form of شد ('it became/it went/it happened') used here to mean the letter 'went' or 'came' (past action); تا باختر uses تا...باختر as 'as far as/to the West'. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s318-g92: به جایی ('to a place') introduces a destination; که is the relative 'where/that'. بد modifies the following noun (adjective before noun in the classical/poetic word order) giving 'evil/bad'; مهتری here is read as 'chief/elder' (a person). با + گهر 'with a jewel' indicates the chief possessed or bore a jewel at that place.
segments.s318-g93: که introduces a clause ('where/that'); روی زمین is a locative phrase 'on the surface/face of the earth' (روی here = 'upon/on'). از marks the source 'from', and بد סژدها is 'the evil dragon' with بد as prepositive adjective (poetic order) modifying اژدها.
segments.s318-g94: به شمشیر کیخسرو uses به instrumentally ('by/with') to mark the means: 'by Kay Khosrow's sword' (شمشیر + proper name in genitive relation). آمد رها is a poetic compound: آمد ('came') + رها ('free/unbound') together convey 'was set free' or 'came freed' — a passive/resultive sense.
segments.s318-g95: به here marks agency/means ('by' or 'through'). نیروی یزدان is a possessive/construct phrase 'the power/strength of God' (نیروی = 'strength' with the implied ezāfe to یزدان), and پیروزگر is an epithet 'victorious/triumphant' qualifying that divine power.
segments.s318-g96: نیاسود is a negative past compound ('نیـ' + آسود) meaning 'did not rest' (he did not take repose). و = 'and'. نگشاد combines the negative prefix نـ with گشاد (here verbal) meaning 'did not open/loosen'; هرگز 'never' intensifies the negation; کمر ('belt, girdle') is the object whose loosening is being denied—idiomatically 'his belt never loosened' = he never relaxed.
segments.s318-g65: The segment begins with the poetic preposition ز (z) meaning 'from', followed by a simple coordination of three proper names (تور و فریدون و سلم). The final word سترگ is an adjective placed after سلم in the archaic/poetic order to qualify Salm as 'mighty/large' (here functioning attributively).
segments.s318-g66: Another prepositional ز introduces Iraj as another source/lineage. که begins a relative clause 'who/that', and بد here is used adjectivally as 'ill/ill-fated' (classical usage). پادشاه بزرگ is a noun + postpositive adjective 'king great' (English: 'great king'); the relative clause qualifies Iraj.
segments.s318-g67: به + noun (به دژخیم) marks the indirect object/recipient 'to the torturer'. فرمود is a past-tense reportive verb 'he commanded/said', and تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / to the effect that'. تیغ تیز is a noun phrase 'sharp sword' where the adjective تیز follows the noun in typical Persian order.
segments.s318-g68: کشید is the past tense of 'کشیدن' here meaning 'drew / pulled (a sword)'; it coordinates with the next verb via و 'and'. بیامد is past 'came'. دلی پر ستیز is a noun phrase with دلی (a heart-related adjective/noun) + پر 'full (of)' + ستیز 'strife', yielding 'came with a heart full of strife'.
segments.s318-g69: میان here indicates 'in the middle / mid-' (miān ... be do nim کرد = 'cut in two'), followed by the title سپهبد (spahbed). به دو نیم کرد is a causative construction: به + measure (دو نیم) + کرد 'made/did', i.e. 'made into two halves / cut in two'.
segments.s318-g70: سپه را uses the object marker را to mark 'the army' as definite object; همه دل پر از بیم کرد is a causative clause 'made all (their) hearts full of fear' where همه modifies دل (the whole heart/entirely), پر از = 'full of', and کرد is the verb 'made/caused'.
segments.s318-g71: به هم is an idiom meaning 'together / onto one another'. برفگندندشان is a verb with the enclitic object شان 'them' ('they cast/laid them upon/they piled them'); همچو کوه is a simile 'like a mountain', with همچو preceding the simile noun کوه.
segments.s318-g72: ز هر سو 'from every side' is a standard prepositional phrase (ز + هر + سو), به دور ایستاده is a participial phrase 'standing around', and گروه is the subject 'a group / troop'. The phrase order places the locative/emphatic phrases before the nominal 'group' for poetic effect.
segments.s318-g1: 'بیامد' is a past-tense verb 'came' placed before its subject (poetic inversion). 'جهاندار' is the subject 'the sovereign/conqueror'. 'با تیغ تیز' is a prepositional phrase 'with a sharp sword' (با = 'with', تیغ = 'sword', تیز = post-positive adjective 'sharp').
segments.s318-g2: Two coordinated nominal descriptions modify the sovereign: 'سری پر ز کینه' = 'a head full of hatred' where 'پر' after the noun means 'full (of)' and 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'of/from'; 'دلی پر ستیز' = 'a heart full of strife' (دلی here functions as 'heart', and ستیز is the noun 'strife').
segments.s318-g3: 'چنین' is an adverb 'thus/so' introducing reported speech. 'گفت' is the past verb 'said' (here used as a verb even though the listed senses were different). 'بی‌دولت' is an epithet before the name 'افراسیاب', meaning 'dispossessed/powerless'. Word order: adverb + verb + epithet + name.
segments.s318-g4: 'که' introduces the content clause ('that' / 'that which'); 'این روز را' is 'this day' with the object marker 'را'; 'دیده بودم' is a compound past (past participle 'دیده' + auxiliary 'بودم') meaning 'I had seen' or 'I saw' in narrative past; 'به خواب' indicates the state/place 'in a dream' (به + خواب). The verb/auxiliary appears at the end as normal for Persian subordinate clauses.
segments.s318-g5: 'سپهر بلند' is the subject 'the lofty sky/heavens'. 'ار' is an archaic conjunction ('if/when') used in poetry; 'فراوان' functions adverbially here ('abundantly' / 'in large measure') modifying the verb; 'کشید' is the past verb 'drew/pulled' (image of drawing back a covering). Verb-final order and the archaic 'ار' give a temporal/conditional sense.
segments.s318-g6: 'همان پرده' = 'that very curtain/veil' (همان emphatic determiner) is the subject; 'رازها' = 'the secrets' is the object (plural); 'بردرید' is the past verb 'tore apart' (object preceding verb is normal poetic/Persian order). The clause describes the veil revealing/taking away secrets.
segments.s318-g7: 'به آواز گفت' = 'he said aloud' (به + noun expressing manner); 'ای' is the vocative 'O'; 'بد کینه جوی' is a compound vocative epithet 'evil / wicked, seeker of hatred' where 'جوی' is the 'seeker' element attached to 'کینه'. Word order: manner phrase + verb + vocative.
segments.s318-g8: 'چرا' asks 'why'; 'کشت' here refers to 'killing' (the act of slaying); 'خواهی' is the present/future-form 'you will / you wish' asking about intent; 'نیا را' is the definite object (Niyâ) marked by 'را'; 'بگوی' is an imperative/subjunctive 'say!' addressing the addressee — overall: 'Why do you intend to kill Niyâ? Tell (us)!' The poetic word order separates the elements but the intended interrogative sequence is clear.
segments.s318-g41: ز (here = 'because of') introduces a cause: ز کردار بد = 'because of (his) bad deeds'. بر تنش is a prepositional phrase 'upon his body/person' where ش is the enclitic possessive 'his'. بد precedes رسید and functions adverbially/qualifying the verb ('ill' or 'badly'), and رسید is a past intransitive verb meaning 'arrived / befell' — together 'بد رسید' = 'ill befell'. Word order is causal phrase + locative/upon‑phrase + verbal predicate (poetic inversion).
segments.s318-g42: مجو‌ای is the negative imperative 'مَجو' ('do not seek') with the vocative particle ای attached for address. پسر is a vocative 'O son'. بند بد را کلید is a poetic object phrase: را marks the definite/defocused element ('بند بد') and the head noun کلید ('key') follows, so the whole phrase is the object of مجو ('do not seek the key of the evil bond').
segments.s318-g43: چو here introduces a conditional/temporal clause ('if/when'). جویی (2nd‑person present/subjunctive form) = 'you seek/ask'. بدانی is an imperative/hortative 2nd‑person form 'know' (used to introduce what follows). که introduces the content clause. از کار بد is a prepositional phrase 'from a bad deed'. The clause thus reads 'If you ask, know that from a bad deed...'.
segments.s318-g44: به فرجام = 'to/into the outcome' (adverbial: 'in the end'). بر + بدکنش = 'upon the evildoer' (بر governs the person afflicted). بد modifies the verb and yields 'ill/badly', and رسد is the present/subjunctive/generic verb 'befalls/comes to' (used here as a general truth about what happens). The sequence gives 'in the end ill befalls the evildoer'.
segments.s318-g45: سپهبد is the head noun; که introduces a relative clause ('who...'). با + فر یزدان = 'with the divine farr' (با here = 'having/with' — an ablative/possessional sense common in older/poetic Persian). بود is the copula/past 'was', so the clause says the spahbed 'was with/possessed the divine glory'.
segments.s318-g46: همه خشم او is the subject phrase 'all his anger' (او following noun marks possession: 'his anger'). بند و زندان بود is a nominal predicate 'was fetter and prison' — a metaphorical statement: his entire wrath constituted bonds and imprisonment. Note final بود as the copula in past/poetic style.
segments.s318-g47: چو = 'when/if' introduces the temporal/conditional clause. خونریز گردَد is a compound where خونریز (blood‑shedding/bloody) and گردد (becomes) form 'becomes bloodthirsty'. بماند ('remains') is a present/subjunctive form used as a general/persistent predicate and is followed by the adjective نژند ('base/vile'), so 'when he becomes bloodthirsty he remains base.'
segments.s318-g48: مکافات is the subject 'retribution/punishment'. یابد (3rd‑person present/subjunctive/general) = 'finds/receives' (here 'will receive' or 'falls upon'). ز چرخ بلند is a prepositional phrase 'from the high/lofty heaven/fate' (چرخ used literarily for sky or fate, modified by بلند). The clause means 'retribution will come from the lofty heaven/fate.'
segments.s318-g57: کشیدندش is a past, 3rd-person plural verb (کشیدند) with the enclitic object -ش = 'him/it' (they dragged him). از پیش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from/in the presence of' (here: 'before'). دژخیم is a compound noun 'torturer/oppressor'. زار follows the noun and functions as an adjective/adverb 'wretched, pitiable' describing the person dragged or his condition.
segments.s318-g58: به بند گران is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into/under heavy fetters' (به + بند 'fetter' modified by گران 'heavy/grievous'); here به has a directional/into sense. و links the two coordinated phrases. به بد روزگار literally 'into bad time/fate' (به + بد روزگار), with بد 'unfavorable' and روزگار used in the sense of 'fate'—together meaning 'to ill fortune' or 'to a cruel fate'.
segments.s318-g59: ابا here is an archaic/discourse marker introducing a contrastive/topicive clause (classical usage similar to 'but' or 'as for'); in the provided sense list it is closest to the notion of 'refusal/reluctance' but functions syntactically to set up the following phrase. روزبانان is the plural agent 'heralds/announcers'. مردم‌کشان is an epithet 'people‑slayers' attached to them.
segments.s318-g60: چنانچون is a simile marker 'just as' or 'such as'. بود is the past copula 'was' (past tense). مردم is 'the people' and بدنشان 'their bodies' (بدن + enclitic -شان 'their'); the clause states a comparison: 'their bodies were like the people's.' Word order places the simile marker before the copula and the possessed noun after it.
segments.s318-g61: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. در پیش 'in the presence of / before' (در + پیش) introduces the location 'before Kay Khosrow'. آمد is the simple past 'came' and here forms a causative/state idiom with به + noun: آمد به درد = 'was seized by pain' or 'was stricken with sorrow/pain' (آمد used intransitively with به marking the resulting state).
segments.s318-g62: ببارید is the simple past of the verb 'to fall/rain down'; used metaphorically here ('rained down') about drops. خون is the noun 'blood' and بر رخ is the prepositional phrase 'upon the face' (بر = on/upon, رخ = face). لاژورد (lapis/lazuli) follows رخ to specify the face's color/appearance (literary, 'his lapis‑blue face').
segments.s318-g63: شهنشاه ایران = 'the Shahanshah (king) of Iran' (title + genitive). زبان برگشاد is an idiomatic expression: زبان here = 'speech/utterance' and برگشاد (past form of برگشودن) = 'opened', so 'opened his tongue' = 'he began to speak.' Word order places the object/phrase 'زبان' before the verb form برگشاد.
segments.s318-g64: و زآن 'and from that' (زآن = 'from that/thereupon') links the speech to what follows. تشت و خنجر are two direct objects 'the basin and the dagger'. بسی is an adverb 'much/many' (here 'much/again and again' or 'at length'), and کرد یاد is a composite verbal expression 'made mention / remembered' (کرد + یاد = 'caused/reminded/made remembrance of' — i.e., 'recalled/mentioned').

sentences.s318-s53: After that he handed (her) over to Ramash; the troop of Khosrow-nezhad departed.
sentences.s318-s54: Whoever was of the Zar-sab lineage came to Azar-Gashasp's iwan.
sentences.s318-s55: For forty days who was with King Kavus — he was always with Ramash, the lute, and wine.
sentences.s318-s56: When the new moon shone brightly in the sky, a golden diadem (was) upon the head of the new king.
sentences.s318-s57: The nobles turned their faces toward Pars, at ease from battle and from dispute.
sentences.s318-s58: In every city into which they came by the road, an assembly of men was formed before the court.
sentences.s318-s59: The king, whose head (countenance) was broad as the full moons, became a wealthy and pious man.
sentences.s318-s5: He answered thus: "O ill-doer, deserving of admonition and reproach."
sentences.s318-s6: For the sake of your brother's life I will first say that he never sought to bring calamity upon the guests.
sentences.s318-s7: Another was Nowzar, that famed king, who, sprung from Irāj's seed, proved an ill remembrance to him.
sentences.s318-s8: You struck off his neck with a sharp sword; you thus provoked an upheaval in the world.
sentences.s318-s17: Now is a god‑sent day; the punishment for evil comes from God.
sentences.s318-s18: He smote his neck with an Indian sword.
sentences.s318-s19: His white beard and hair were stained ruby by the blood.
sentences.s318-s20: The imperial seat was left empty of him; the fair days came to an end for him.
sentences.s318-s41: He also bestowed robes upon those mobeds, and gave dirhams, dinars, and many other things.
sentences.s318-s42: Within the city's inner quarters, whoever was a dervish — or if he ate by his own exertion.
sentences.s318-s43: He likewise scattered treasure there; by giving and grants he made the whole world his servant.
sentences.s318-s44: After that he sat upon the Kian throne; he opened his court and closed his lips.
sentences.s318-s37: When, by God's will, the king's wishes were fulfilled, the king hastened from the sea toward Khan Azar.
sentences.s318-s38: They threw great quantities of gold onto the fire; at the Zamzam they repeatedly cried 'afarin' (praise).
sentences.s318-s39: They remained a day and a night at (his) feet, in the presence of the world-judge and guide.
sentences.s318-s40: When the treasure-laden Ki-Khosrow arrived, he bestowed a treasure upon Azar-Gashasp.
sentences.s318-s49: He revived Siavash's soul, and made the world a servant by justice and generosity.
sentences.s318-s50: He continually bestowed things upon the dervish, upon the devotee, and upon his own people.
sentences.s318-s51: After that the king of the world said thus: “O famed and fortunate nobles!”
sentences.s318-s52: Women and little children went outside; Khorshahā and Rāmash went to the hamun (the plain).
sentences.s318-s25: Thus the mobed said to Bahram the Swift, "Do not spill the blood of the innocent."
sentences.s318-s26: If you want your crown to remain in its place, use no means except those that are gentle and pure‑minded.
sentences.s318-s27: Pay heed to what the crown itself said to the head: 'May it be paired with your brain, O wise head.'
sentences.s318-s28: To Garsivaz came Niyâ from the affair, with two pale cheeks and a heart full of alchemy.
sentences.s318-s13: Stay so that I may see your mother alive; then read these tales.
sentences.s318-s14: He said to him, 'If you desired my mother, why did you kindle a fire upon my head?'
sentences.s318-s15: My father was a stranger, and I was hidden — what harm came to me from you in the world?
sentences.s318-s16: You stole the head of sovereignty so that the crown ... — it began to weep wretchedly to him, and the ivory throne ...
sentences.s318-s9: There was no one else like Siavash; no one among the guests had seen a rider like him.
sentences.s318-s10: You cut off his head like a sheep's head; then you departed from the lofty sky.
sentences.s318-s11: You hastened swiftly in evil conduct; now you have received the retribution for that evil.
sentences.s318-s12: He said to him, 'O King, what has been, has been; now you must hear my story.'
sentences.s318-s45: They wrote letters to every country, to every notable and every chief.
sentences.s318-s46: From the East the letter went to the West, to a place where an evil chief had a jewel.
sentences.s318-s47: And that jewel, upon the face of the earth, was freed from the evil dragon by Kay Khosrow's sword.
sentences.s318-s48: By the victorious power of God he did not rest, and his belt never loosened.
sentences.s318-s33: From Tur and Fereydun and mighty Salm, from Iraj — who was an ill-fated great king.
sentences.s318-s34: He ordered the torturer to draw a sharp sword; he drew it and came, his heart full of strife.
sentences.s318-s35: He cut the spahbed in two, and made the whole army's heart full of fear.
sentences.s318-s36: They heaped them together like a mountain; groups stood all around on every side.
sentences.s318-s1: The sovereign came with a sharp sword, a head full of hatred and a heart full of strife.
sentences.s318-s2: Thus spoke the dispossessed Afrasiab: 'I had seen this day in a dream.'
sentences.s318-s3: When the lofty heavens drew back their abundant covering, that very veil tore apart the secrets.
sentences.s318-s4: He cried aloud, 'O wicked seeker of grudges, why do you wish to kill Niyâ? Tell (me)!'
sentences.s318-s21: Because of evil deeds ill befell his body; do not seek, O son, the key of an evil bond.
sentences.s318-s22: If you ask, know that from an evil deed, in the end a bad fate will befall the evildoer.
sentences.s318-s23: The spahbed, who was endowed with the divine farr, had all his wrath as fetters and a prison.
sentences.s318-s24: When he becomes blood‑shedding he remains base; retribution will come from the high heaven.
sentences.s318-s29: They dragged him before the torturer, the wretched, and put him into heavy chains and into an ill fate.
sentences.s318-s30: As for the heralds — the slayers of men — their bodies were just like the people's.
sentences.s318-s31: When he came before Kay Khosrow he was stricken with pain; blood rained upon his lapis‑blue face.
sentences.s318-s32: The Shahanshah of Iran opened his mouth, and from that he spoke much of the basin and the dagger.
